description.summary: A nocturnal spirit in Thai folklore said to sit on a sleeper's chest, paralyzing them and causing breathing difficulty. The name is used colloquially in Thailand for the experience of sleep paralysis itself.
description.full: Phi Am (Thai: ผีอำ) is a spirit in Thai folklore that visits sleepers at night and presses down on the chest, immobilizing the victim and obstructing breathing. The phenomenon is part of a global family of sleep-paralysis traditions — analogous to the Old Hag of Newfoundland, the Mara of Scandinavia, kanashibari in Japan, bangungot in the Philippines, and khmaoch sangkat in Cambodia — in which a supernatural figure is held responsible for the immobility and dread that accompanies a particular sleep state. In Thailand the term has become so deeply associated with the underlying experience that 'phi am' is used by both lay speakers and medical practitioners to mean sleep paralysis. Modern sleep medicine identifies the phenomenon as a parasomnia in which atonia of REM sleep persists into wakefulness, frequently accompanied by hypnopompic hallucinations of presence, weight, and pressure.

etymology.origin: Thai. 'Phi' (ผี) is the generic Thai word for ghost or spirit; 'am' (อำ) is a verb meaning 'to press down', 'to hold in place', or 'to suppress'.
etymology.meaning: 'The ghost that presses down' or 'the suppressing ghost', describing the felt experience of being pinned to the bed.
etymology.first_use: Vernacular Thai usage; documented in 20th-century Thai folklore writing and in cross-cultural sleep-paralysis literature.

related_phenomena.name: Khmaoch Sangkat
related_phenomena.description: Cambodian/Lao 'ghost that pushes you down' tradition

features.feature: Largely invisible / felt rather than seen
features.description: Witnesses typically describe pressure, weight, and presence rather than a clearly visualized figure
Sensation of a heavy entity sitting on the sleeper's chest, sometimes the liver, restricting breathing
Some victims report unexplained bruises or scrapes after an episode

physical.morphology: Phi Am is generally felt rather than seen; vernacular and modern-media depictions sometimes give it a shadowy human form, but the tradition emphasizes the sensation of being pressed down rather than a precise body.

abilities.description: Holds the sleeper immobile, unable to move limbs, speak, or cry out
Presses on the chest so the sleeper feels short of breath
Associated with vivid dread, sensed presences, and hypnopompic imagery
Folk reports of bruises or scrapes following an attack

behavior.diet: Not described as physically feeding; in some retellings characterized as drawing on the sleeper's vital force
patterns: Visits sleepers at night, typically as they fall asleep or wake
Sits on the chest, sometimes the liver, immobilizing the victim
Episode ends abruptly when the sleeper succeeds in moving or crying out

range.description: Thailand, with cognate traditions throughout mainland Southeast Asia. Phi Am is part of the broader Thai phi cosmology and reflects a worldwide pattern of sleep-paralysis folklore.

first_recorded.source: Thai oral tradition; documented in 20th-century folklore writing and in modern Thai sleep-medicine usage

temporal.seasonal_patterns: No reported seasonality; reports follow the underlying frequency of sleep paralysis.

origins.cultural_roots: Embedded in the Thai phi (ผี) tradition — a vast cosmology of spirits associated with places, conditions, and life events. Phi Am specifically personifies the experience of sleep paralysis.
origins.earliest_accounts: Traditional oral folklore; modern documentation appears in Thai folklore studies and cross-cultural sleep-paralysis literature.
origins.folklore_connections: Belongs to the global 'night hag' family of sleep-paralysis spirits — Mara, Old Hag, kanashibari, bangungot, khmaoch sangkat — in which a supernatural being is blamed for the experience of waking immobility.

timeline.event: Phi Am appears in Thai oral tradition as the personification of sleep paralysis
Term 'phi am' enters everyday and clinical Thai usage as a name for sleep paralysis itself
timeline.significance: Folkloric term becomes a medical colloquialism

history.modern_era: Phi Am remains widely known in contemporary Thailand as both a folkloric figure and a synonym for sleep paralysis. It appears in Thai comics, films, and television. Cross-cultural studies of sleep paralysis (e.g. Jalal & Hinton) cite phi am alongside kanashibari, bangungot, and similar traditions as evidence that sleep paralysis is interpreted through culturally specific spirit narratives across the world.

documentary.name: Thai phi (ผี) folklore tradition
documentary.description: Body of Thai oral and written folklore in which Phi Am appears as the personification of sleep paralysis.
documentary.analysis: Documented in folkloristics; the underlying physiological phenomenon (sleep paralysis) is well-established in sleep medicine.

paranormal.name: Spirit attack
paranormal.description: Traditional interpretation: a wandering spirit (phi) takes advantage of the sleeper's vulnerable state to press down on the chest and feed on or torment them.
evidence_cited: Subjective experience of presence, weight, and paralysis

scientific.name: Sleep paralysis (REM atonia intrusion)
scientific.description: A parasomnia in which the muscle atonia of REM sleep persists into early wakefulness, leaving the sleeper conscious but unable to move. Frequently accompanied by hypnopompic hallucinations of presence, pressure, and dread, which the brain interprets through culturally available imagery.
evidence_cited: Consistent symptom cluster across radically different cultures

skeptical.name: Cultural framing of a universal experience
skeptical.description: Phi Am is not a distinct entity but the Thai cultural script applied to a worldwide neurological phenomenon, in the same way that the Old Hag, Mara, or kanashibari are local scripts for the same underlying experience.
evidence_cited: Near-identical symptomology across cultures with no historical contact
Consistent prevalence rates of sleep paralysis (~8–30% lifetime) regardless of belief

stories: First-person accounts of being held down and unable to breathe at night, often retold in Thai households as warnings or family memory
folklore.beliefs: Phi Am is regarded as a real, if minor, supernatural threat in popular Thai belief; the term has become common usage for sleep paralysis itself.

literature: Thai folklore compendia and Thai-language ghost-story collections
film_tv: Phi Am and other phi figures recur in Thai horror cinema and television
other: Frequently depicted in Thai comics and online horror media

researchers.name: Baland Jalal
researchers.affiliation: University of Cambridge / Harvard Medical School (sleep & cross-cultural psychiatry)
researchers.contribution: Cross-cultural studies of sleep paralysis and its supernatural interpretations, including Southeast Asian traditions

researchers.name: Devon E. Hinton
researchers.contribution: Research on culturally specific sleep paralysis experiences in Cambodia and the wider region

research.ongoing_studies: Sleep paralysis remains an active area of cross-cultural psychiatry and sleep medicine, with Thai phi am cited as a representative Southeast Asian case.

sources.title: Ghosts in Thai culture — Phi Am
Sleep Paralysis in Brazilian Folklore and Other Cultures: A Brief Review
